perturbation.original_text: The Confidant must take all reasonable precautions to avoid unauthorised disclosure and protect against unauthorised use o f the Confidential Information , such precautions to be at least as stringent as those used by the Confidant for the protection of the Confidant ’s own confidential information and always in accordance with the Privacy Act 1988 .
perturbation.changed_text: The Confidant must take all reasonable precautions to avoid unauthorised disclosure and protect against unauthorised use o f the Confidential Information , such precautions to be at least as stringent as those used by the Confidant for the protection of the Confidant ’s own confidential information. The Confidant is not required to comply with the Privacy Act 1988.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a conflict by stating that the Confidant is not required to comply with the Privacy Act 1988, which is a mandatory law for handling personal information in Australia.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Privacy Act 1988 (Cth) establishes a framework of principles and obligations for handling personal information. By stating that the Confidant is not required to comply with the Privacy Act, the modified text directly contradicts Australian law, rendering the clause unenforceable and potentially exposing the Confidant to legal penalties for privacy breaches.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: The Privacy Act | OAIC We use cookies on this site We use cookies to analyse traffic and to improve your browsing experience on our website. To find out more, read our privacy policy . Close Skip to main content The Privacy Act The Privacy Act 1988 was introduced to promote and protect the privacy of individuals and to regulate how Australian Government agencies and organisations with an annual turnover of more than $3 million, and some other organisations, handle personal information. The Privacy Act includes 13 Australian Privacy Principles (APPs), which apply to some private sector organisations, as well as most Australian Government agencies. Such organisations and agencies are collectively known as ‘APP entities’. The Privacy Act also regulates the privacy component of the consumer credit reporting system, tax file numbers, and health and medical research. History of the Privacy Act Changes to the Privacy Act 1988 since it began, in brief Rights and responsibilities Who has rights under the Privacy Act and which organisations and agencies the Privacy Act covers The Australian Government is conducting a review of the Privacy Act, see our submissions to the review ( Privacy Act Review Issues Paper submission and Privacy Act Review Discussion Paper submission ) and our Research publications on the Privacy Act . Audits and investigations Commissioner-initiated investigations Why we open a Commissioner-initiated investigation and our current investigations Privacy assessment powers We audit privacy practices of APP entities to help ensure personal information is protected Associated codes, regulations, rules and guidelines Credit reporting Consumer credit reporting under the Privacy Act, which is supported by a regulation and code Health and medical research When a health service provider can use or disclose genetic information, and when health and medical research can use personal information Privacy regulations Regulations issued under the Privacy Act Rules and guidelines Legally binding rules and guidelines we've made or approved under the Privacy Act Tax file numbers Privacy (Tax File Number) Rule 2015 and other obligations Did you find this helpful? Yes No We'd love to hear more! Please tell us more Rate
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the Privacy Act 1988, an Australian law designed to protect individual privacy. The original text explicitly states compliance with the Privacy Act 1988, while the changed text explicitly states that the Confidant is not required to comply. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that the Privacy Act 1988 was introduced to 'promote and protect the privacy of individuals' and regulates how organizations handle personal information. By exempting the Confidant from complying with this Act, the modified contract creates a direct and serious contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Following any request by NostraData , the Confidant must immediately provide to NostraData all Confidential Information and certify that no Confidential Information is retained by the Confidant . The obligation to return Confidential Information includ es the obligation to return any and all Confidential Information in physical, electronic or other tangible form.
perturbation.changed_text: Following any request by NostraData, the Confidant must provide to NostraData all Confidential Information within 90 days and certify that no Confidential Information is retained by the Confidant. The obligation to return Confidential Information includes the obligation to return any and all Confidential Information in physical, electronic or other tangible form.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a delay in returning the confidential information, conflicting with the 'immediately' requirement, which can be problematic under general contractual obligations requiring timely performance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law Principles (Duty of Good Faith)
perturbation.law_citation: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing 'immediately' to 'within 90 days', the modified text contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, which requires parties to a contract to act honestly and reasonably. Delaying the return of confidential information for 90 days could be seen as a breach of this duty, especially if it causes harm to NostraData.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The change from 'immediately' to 'within 90 days' introduces a contradiction by potentially violating the duty of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'good faith' as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'faithful performance of duties.' Delaying the return of confidential information for 90 days, as allowed by the modified text, may not constitute 'faithful performance' or 'honest dealing,' particularly if it harms NostraData. The original contract required immediate action, whereas the modified version allows for a significant delay that could be construed as a breach of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: At the conclusion of the Datathon the Confi dant must permanently delete all data provided by NostraData .
perturbation.changed_text: At the conclusion of the Datathon the Confidant may retain all data provided by NostraData indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows indefinite retention of data, potentially conflicting with privacy laws and data retention requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Australian Privacy Principle 11 - Security of personal information
perturbation.law_explanation: Australian Privacy Principle 11 requires organizations to take reasonable steps to protect personal information they hold from misuse, interference and loss, as well as unauthorised access, modification or disclosure.  It also requires them to destroy or de-identify personal information if it is no longer needed for any purpose for which the information may be used or disclosed under the APPs. Allowing the indefinite retention of the data goes against these principles, as the data may contain sensitive information and holding it indefinitely increases the risk of a data breach.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the permanent deletion of data, ensuring compliance with data protection principles. The changed text, however, allows for the indefinite retention of data. The Law Explanation describes how indefinite retention conflicts with Australian Privacy Principle 11, which requires organizations to destroy or de-identify personal information when it is no longer needed. Although the scraped snippets do not directly support this explanation, the Law explanation's claim that the change goes against data protection principles is sufficient to justify a YES, as the contract is now inconsistent with a legally recognized privacy obligation.

perturbation.original_text: This deed is governed by the laws of the State of Victoria , Australia .
perturbation.changed_text: This deed is governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, USA.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware introduces jurisdictional issues if the Datathon and NostraData are based in Victoria, Australia. It can affect which laws apply to contract interpretation and enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Conflict of Laws (Choice of Law)
perturbation.law_explanation: Although parties are generally free to choose the governing law, courts may not honor the choice if the chosen state has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction, and application of the chosen law would be contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue.  If the parties and transaction are centered in Victoria, Australia, a Delaware choice of law clause may not be enforceable, especially regarding matters of data protection and privacy relevant to the location of the activity and parties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law from Victoria, Australia, to Delaware, USA. The Law Explanation asserts that such a change might be unenforceable if Delaware has no substantial relationship to the parties or transaction and if applying Delaware law contradicts fundamental policies of a state with a materially greater interest (e.g., Victoria, Australia). The Perturbation Explanation highlights jurisdictional issues and impacts on contract interpretation and enforcement arising from this change. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'conflict of laws' as a difference between jurisdictions where the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law is applied, emphasizing the importance of determining the appropriate law. Because the original text specified Victoria and the changed text specifies Delaware, it creates a conflict of law issue that may not be enforceable.
